activities_projects.title: Production Factor Analysis
activities_projects.objective: Analyze how different production units balance the four factors of production
steps: Select two different production units: a small-scale jaggery maker and a large wheat farm
Interview or research the ratio of manual labor to machine capital in both
Determine which unit is 'labor-intensive' and which is 'capital-intensive'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the substitution of labor with capital in production

activities_projects.title: Yield Calculation and Comparison
activities_projects.objective: Mathematically compare productivity of traditional vs. modern farming
steps: Use the textbook data: Traditional yield (1300 kg/ha) vs. HYV yield (3200 kg/ha)
Calculate the percentage increase in production for a 2-hectare farm
Plot a bar graph showing the surplus available for sale after a family consumes 400 kg
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies mathematical skills to understand economic surplus
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of calculations and graphical representation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Irrigation and Power Dynamics
activities_projects.objective: Examine how electricity transformed the socio-economic status of farmers
steps: Trace the timeline of irrigation in Palampur from Persian wheels to electric tubewells
Discuss how the arrival of electricity allowed for a third crop (Potato)
Analyze why some farmers still depend on others for water despite the availability of electricity
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the role of infrastructure in rural development
activities_projects.evaluation: Coherent explanation of the link between power and profit (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Green Revolution Sustainability Audit
activities_projects.objective: Critically assess the long-term environmental costs of modern agriculture
steps: List the chemical inputs required for HYV seeds mentioned in the chapter
Create a 'Problem-Cause-Solution' chart for groundwater depletion and soil fertility
Present arguments for why natural resources, once destroyed, are difficult to restore
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Develops a critical perspective on environmental sustainability
activities_projects.evaluation: Quality of the sustainability report (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Land Distribution Gini-Analysis
activities_projects.objective: Understand the impact of unequal land distribution on village economy
steps: Using the Palampur data (450 families), create a pie chart of land ownership
Categorize: 150 landless, 240 small farmers (<2 hectares), and 60 medium/large farmers
Write a report on how land size affects a farmer's ability to take bank loans
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Visualizes social inequality and its economic consequences
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the pie chart and logical report (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Labor Market Roleplay
activities_projects.objective: Understand the determinants of wages in an unorganized rural market
steps: Simulate a negotiation between a landless laborer and a large farmer during harvest season
Introduce variables: government minimum wage (₹300) vs. actual market rate (₹160)
Identify why laborers accept wages below the legal minimum
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the concept of 'Labor Surplus' and its effect on wages
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to articulate market pressures during roleplay (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Capital Formation Case Study
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the cycle of wealth for large farmers vs. the cycle of debt for small farmers
steps: Create two flowcharts: 'Savita’s Loan Cycle' and 'Tejpal Singh’s Reinvestment Cycle'
Trace how Tejpal uses surplus to buy tractors or lend to others at high interest
Identify where the 'Human Capital' (skills/education) could break Savita's cycle
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between wealth accumulation and debt traps
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of the comparative flowcharts (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Non-Farm Entrepreneurship Plan
activities_projects.objective: Design a small business model based on village resources
steps: Select a non-farm activity (e.g., Mishrilal’s jaggery or Kareem’s computer center)
List the 'Working Capital' needed for one month of operation
Explain the 'risk factor' involved and how it differs from farming risks
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies entrepreneurial thinking to rural economic scenarios
activities_projects.evaluation: Feasibility and detail of the business plan (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Market Linkages Investigation
activities_projects.objective: Understand the importance of transport and trade in rural-urban connectivity
steps: Map the connection between Palampur, Raiganj (large village), and Shahpur (town)
List the goods flowing from village to town and vice versa
Identify how improved transport creates new jobs like rickshaw pulling or truck driving
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains the interdependence of villages and towns
activities_projects.evaluation: Identification of trade routes and job creation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Human Capital Debate
activities_projects.objective: Argue the importance of health and education in increasing production
steps: Read the section on Kareem’s computer class for village students
Prepare a debate: 'Is education more important than land for the future of Palampur?'
List three ways an educated villager contributes more to the economy than an unskilled one
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes the importance of human capital in production
activities_projects.evaluation: Strength of arguments and reasoning (10 marks)
